800:    SUBROUTINE nnp_scale_acsf(nnp, ind, dsymdxyz, stress, active_atoms, n_active)


[...]


814:       IF (nnp%center_acsf) THEN


815:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


816:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = &


817:                (nnp%rad(ind)%y(j) - nnp%rad(ind)%loc_av(j))


818:          END DO


819:          off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


820:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


821:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = &


822:                (nnp%ang(ind)%y(j) - nnp%ang(ind)%loc_av(j))


823:          END DO


824: 


825:          IF (nnp%scale_acsf) THEN


826:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


827:                nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = &


828:                   nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j)*nnp%rad(ind)%scale_factor(j) + nnp%scmin


829:             END DO


830:             off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


831:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


832:                nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = &


833:                   nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off)*nnp%ang(ind)%scale_factor(j) + nnp%scmin


834:             END DO


835:          END IF


836:       ELSE IF (nnp%scale_acsf) THEN


837:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


838:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = &


839:                (nnp%rad(ind)%y(j) - nnp%rad(ind)%loc_min(j))*nnp%rad(ind)%scale_factor(j) &


840:                + nnp%scmin


841:          END DO


842:          off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


843:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


844:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = &


845:                (nnp%ang(ind)%y(j) - nnp%ang(ind)%loc_min(j))*nnp%ang(ind)%scale_factor(j) &


846:                + nnp%scmin


847:          END DO


848:       ELSE IF (nnp%scale_sigma_acsf) THEN


849:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


850:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = &


851:                (nnp%rad(ind)%y(j) - nnp%rad(ind)%loc_av(j))*nnp%rad(ind)%inv_sigma_factor(j) &


852:                + nnp%scmin


853:          END DO


854:          off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


855:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


856:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = &


857:                (nnp%ang(ind)%y(j) - nnp%ang(ind)%loc_av(j))*nnp%ang(ind)%inv_sigma_factor(j) &


858:                + nnp%scmin


859:          END DO


860:       ELSE


861:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


862:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = nnp%rad(ind)%y(j)


863:          END DO


864:          off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


865:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


866:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = nnp%ang(ind)%y(j)


[...]


873:       IF (PRESENT(dsymdxyz)) THEN


874:          IF (nnp%scale_acsf) THEN


875:             IF (PRESENT(active_atoms) .AND. PRESENT(n_active)) THEN


876:                !$OMP PARALLEL DO COLLAPSE(2) DEFAULT(SHARED) PRIVATE(idx, j, k) SCHEDULE(static)

[...]


949:       IF (PRESENT(stress)) THEN


950:          IF (nnp%scale_acsf) THEN


951:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


952:                stress(:, :, j) = stress(:, :, j)*nnp%rad(ind)%scale_factor(j)


953:             END DO


954:             off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


955:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


956:                stress(:, :, j + off) = stress(:, :, j + off)*nnp%ang(ind)%scale_factor(j)


957:             END DO


958:          ELSE IF (nnp%scale_sigma_acsf) THEN


959:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


960:                stress(:, :, j) = stress(:, :, j)*nnp%rad(ind)%inv_sigma_factor(j)


961:             END DO


962:             off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


963:             DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


964:                stress(:, :, j + off) = stress(:, :, j + off)*nnp%ang(ind)%inv_sigma_factor(j)


965:             END DO


966:          END IF


967:       END IF


968: 


969:    END SUBROUTINE nnp_scale_acsf

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 508.00 to 46.00 cycles (11.04x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 508.00 to 36.38 cycles (13.96x speedup).

Details


All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 508.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 5% of peak store performance is reached (3.47 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

The code analyzed by CQA in that panel excludes loops and represents 0.01% of application time for run run_0
        

          
Source file and linesnnp_acsf.F:800-969

          
Modulecp2k.psmp

        

        
The function is defined in /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:800,814-866,873-941,949-969.

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 508.00 to 46.00 cycles (11.04x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is not vectorized.
Only 11% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).
By vectorizing your function, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 508.00 to 36.38 cycles (13.96x speedup).

Details


All SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).
Since your execution units are vector units, only a vectorized function can use their full power.


Workaround


  • Try another compiler or update/tune your current one
  • Make array accesses unit-stride:

    • If your function streams arrays of structures (AoS), try to use structures of arrays instead (SoA):
do i a(i)%x = b(i)%x (slow, non stride 1) => do i a%x(i) = b%x(i) (fast, stride 1)


Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  

No data for this section

  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

  • __intel_avx_rep_memcpy: 2 occurrences►

Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.
